definition: The third-generation cognitive science framework treating cognition as embodied (constitutively dependent on the body), embedded (constitutively coupled to environment), enacted (constituted in interaction), and extended (using external tools and other minds) — providing the naturalistic worldview in which relevance realization and religio can be situated.

sourcePassage.quote: We're also talking, not only about it being embodied \[and\] embedded, it is inherently a \[transactional\] relation of relevance realization. The world and the organism are co-creating co-determining co-evolving the fittedness.

notes: Vervaeke uses 4E cognition as the worldview within which his response to the meaning crisis is situated — a naturalistic, scientific worldview that nevertheless restores transjective participation.

definition: The Hebraic kind of knowing — the participatory knowing of being on course in the unfolding cosmic story, of one's life being changed by what one is changing as one engages with it — the same word used for sexual intercourse, marking it as a deeply participatory knowing.

sourcePassage.quote: Faith was your sense of "DA'ATH". Faith was your sense that you are in this reciprocal realization: you're "in course". You're "on course". You're involved and evolving with things.

notes: Vervaeke uses Da'ath to recover the original Hebraic meaning of faith — far from credulous assertion, it is participatory courseness.

definition: Plotinus's name for the inexhaustible source of all intelligibility and being — that by which everything is realized, but which itself cannot be known propositionally, only united with through being one.

sourcePassage.quote: So Plotinus calls this The One. He doesn't mean the single. This is that by which reality is realized and our mind realizes reality.

notes: Plotinus uses the metaphor of light: The One is itself invisible but makes seeing possible. Neo from The Matrix is named for Neoplatonism.

definition: The experience of disconnection between agent and arena — the tennis player in the football arena — in which one's actions and projects no longer make sense within a worldview; a fundamental symptom of the meaning crisis.

sourcePassage.quote: You will be like the tennis player trying to play tennis in the football arena. You will start to experience your existence as absurd. It won't make any sense to you.

notes: Camus is the modern philosopher of the absurd; Vervaeke argues that absurdity is what happens when worldview attunement fails.

definition: The creative, sacrificial love that turns non-persons into persons — the love by which a parent loves an infant who cannot yet be a friend or a partner — preceding, permeating, and following individuals; the love Vervaeke argues makes us into persons through being loved before we have earned it.

sourcePassage.quote: This is the love of creation. It's the love that God is demonstrating towards humanity in the way God is an ongoing creation of the open future. So God is creating the future.

notes: Vervaeke identifies God in Christianity with the process of agape itself, drawing on John's epistle. Agape is transjective: it precedes us, flows through us, and we participate in it; we did not make it.

definition: The co-identifying coupling in which the self assumes an identity as an agent while assigning the world an identity as an arena that affords appropriate action — together producing the meta-meaning relation within which all individual meanings are possible.

sourcePassage.quote: what you have, when you have a world view, is you've got this Agent and Arena coupling.

notes: The agent-arena relationship is the locus where all individual projects of meaning gain their significance; its loss is the absurdity that Vervaeke (with Mastropietro and Miscevic) explored in 'Zombies in Western Culture.'

definition: Aristotle's name for the foolish state in which one knows what is right but does the wrong thing anyway — not from ignorance but from insufficient character — refuting the Socratic intellectualism that knowledge of the good entails doing the good.

sourcePassage.quote: Akrasia is when you know what the right thing to do is - you KNOW what the right thing to do is - and we talked about this, remember, with the chocolate cake. But you don't do the right thing.

notes: Aristotle's response to akrasia is the cultivation of character through habituation — the virtual engine.

definition: States in which the salience, presence, and depth landscapes are radically transformed — typically through disruption (sleep deprivation, psychedelics, fasting, ritual) — producing systematic insight of consciousness rather than insight in consciousness.

sourcePassage.quote: an altered state of consciousness is not a... It's not an insight in consciousness it's an insight of consciousness. It's a radical transformation of all of your landscapes, not just this particular problem.

notes: ASCs are commonly rejected as illusory; the puzzle of higher states of consciousness is why some are instead taken as more real than ordinary experience.

definition: The Platonic ascent — the reciprocally accelerating process in which growing contact with reality and growing inner integration mutually feed each other, so self and world simultaneously become more real through a cycle of disclosure, transformation, and deeper disclosure.

sourcePassage.quote: There's a Greek word for this 'ascent' called "Anagoge". This is the "Anagogic", or the "Anagogical" aspect of Plato's idea

notes: Anagoge is one of Vervaeke's most-used technical terms; the opposite of Mark Lewis's reciprocal narrowing. Love (agape) is described as an anagogic process of reciprocal realization.

definition: Agnes Callard's incremental version of transformative experience: a process by which one becomes a different self with different values one does not yet fully possess — proleptically rational, since it must be a kind of rationality even as it transcends current rational ground.

sourcePassage.quote: My aspiring to rationality has to be itself a kind of rationality. That's proleptic rationality, or to use something older. My loving of wisdom. My loving of wisdom. My aspiring to becoming wise cannot itself be an irrational process.

notes: Vervaeke argues aspiration is essential to any non-self-refuting model of rationality — since the aspiration toward becoming rational must itself be rational.

definition: Not a direct action like walking but an optimization of other processes — tuning how you look, listen, or remember so they share a goal (Mole's 'cognitive unison'); central to how salience is modulated and how mindfulness reshapes the salience landscape.

sourcePassage.quote: attention isn't a spotlight. It's a very complex optimisation process. It's really about tuning and getting between too tight and too loose and allow you to becoming intimately involved, conformed to, participating, 'inter-essay' with whatever you're paying attention to.

notes: Vervaeke rejects the spotlight metaphor — following Mole, attention is treated as an optimization-of-other-processes (cognitive unison), which is why mindfulness can train attention by reshaping how looking/listening/remembering co-ordinate.

definition: Following Frankfurt, communication that operates not by lying (asserting falsehoods you know to be false) but by capturing attention through salience disconnected from any concern for truth — and, more crucially, the form of self-deception in which one's own attention is captured by what is super-salient rather than what is true.

sourcePassage.quote: See bullshit, unlike lying, works by making you disinterested, unconcerned with whether or not what is being said is true. When somebody is bullshitting you, they're trying to get you to not find important or, "central", how true the claim is.

notes: Vervaeke uses Frankfurt's term technically. Self-bullshitting is possible (whereas self-lying is not) because attention can be directed in a self-reinforcing loop that makes salience overrun understanding.

definition: Aristotle's theory of knowing in which to know something is to share the same idos (structural-functional organization) with it — a contact epistemology, not a representational one — by which the mind takes on the form of what it knows and is changed in its being thereby.

sourcePassage.quote: So when I know some object or know some thing, my mind takes on the same structural functional organization as the thing such that if I could take that idos from my mind and actualize it in some potential, I could make an instance of the thing

notes: Vervaeke sees the conformity theory as undergoing a major revival in current cognitive science — Dreyfus, Merleau-Ponty, 4E approaches all return to contact epistemology.

definition: The contrast between Aristotle's conformity theory of knowing (knowing as taking on the same idos as the thing) and the modern correspondence theory of truth (knowing as the matching of inner representation to outer fact) — the modern collapse onto the latter cost us participatory knowing.

sourcePassage.quote: Aristotle's theory of knowing there is no distinction - that is \[that\] we \[would\] typically have - between knowing and being.

notes: Vervaeke treats the shift from conformity to correspondence as one of the central losses driving the meaning crisis.

definition: The dynamical machinery that coordinates attention and awareness to optimize the realization of relevant information from a vast informational space — generating salience landscape, presence landscape, and depth landscape — and whose alteration is essential to insight, transformation, and the mystical.

sourcePassage.quote: The core function of consciousness seems to be to help you realize, become aware of, actualize, put into action, 'Relevance': relevant information.

notes: Vervaeke draws on Global Workspace Theory (Baars), Integrated Information Theory (Tononi), and the frame-problem literature to argue that consciousness's function is relevance realization, not the production of propositional content.

definition: The networked nature of human cognition in which problem-solving and meaning-making are accomplished not in isolated individuals but in groups of brains linked by psycho-technologies (language, ritual, literacy, institutions) — making any wisdom tradition fundamentally communal.

sourcePassage.quote: Way before the Internet networked computers together, culture networked brains together in order to provide some of our most powerful problem solving abilities.

notes: Vervaeke uses distributed cognition to argue against modern autodidactic spirituality and for the cooperative network of communities of practice in his proposed religion that is not a religion.

definition: Porteous and Smith's term, picked up by Brian Walsh and Vervaeke, for the destruction of home — not merely of one's house but of the connectedness to place, culture, history, language, and community in which one's identity is constituted; central to the Hellenistic crisis and to modernity.

sourcePassage.quote: Domicide is the destruction of home. Now there's two ways in which Domicide can occur. One, of course, is physical destruction of your house, and that's important, but there's also Cultural Domicide in which you have a house, you have a dwelling, but it is not very much your home.

notes: Domicide is one of Vervaeke's key bridges between Hellenistic meaning crisis (Epicureans, Stoics, Cynics) and our own situation.

definition: Not pain or distress but the Buddhist term for the self-destructive disjointedness of an existence threatened by loss of agency — the wheel off-axle that destroys itself as it turns — the perennial vulnerability of agency to entrapment by its own adaptive machinery.

sourcePassage.quote: What does Dukkha mean? Well the etymology is: imagine you have a wheel, and it's off-center on its axis, so the axle is not properly going through the center of the wheel and as the wheel is turning it's destroying itself.

notes: Vervaeke insists dukkha is not 'all is pain' (the Western mistranslation) but 'all life is threatened with loss of agency.'

definition: A mutually supporting set of psycho-technologies — meditation, contemplation, dialogue, ritual, Lectio Divina, martial practice — that together form a counteractive dynamical system against parasitic processing and reciprocal narrowing, and that requires sapiential community to sustain.

sourcePassage.quote: cultures – and individuals, communities, at multiple scales – what people have developed is they have developed sets of psycho-technologies

notes: Vervaeke argues against modern autodidactic spirituality precisely because these practices require an ecology — they fail or become dangerous in isolation.

definition: The love of becoming one with something — being one with nature, with a beloved, even with a cookie consumed — the love satisfied through consummation and unification.

sourcePassage.quote: Eros is the love that seeks to be one with something.

notes: Distinct from the modern reduction to sexual love. Socrates's ta erotika is about knowing how to love well — what to care about.

definition: Michael Anderson's extension of Gould's biological concept: the cognitive process by which a brain mechanism evolved for one purpose is reused for another — like the tongue exapted for speech, or self-machinery exapted onto the world in mystical experience.

sourcePassage.quote: Now originally this is an idea from biology, but the [work of Michael Anderson](https://www.dropbox.com/s/gbzohaikvbqq5d1/69anderson_synthese_2007.pdf?dl=0) has brought it directly into understanding how the brain operates; how cognition operates.

notes: Exaptation grounds Vervaeke's argument for how shamanic practice could enhance cognition without biological change.

definition: The condition of being simultaneously existentially stuck (unable to engage sensibility transcendence into a new worldview) and existentially stupefied (unable to reason one's way through a transformative decision) — for which gnosis, via ritual serious play and enactive analogy, is the response.

sourcePassage.quote: They're stuck and they're stupefied. And when we're stuck and we're stupefied when we're stuck, we can't imagine how to make an alternative worldview significant. How to make it viable when we're stupefied. We can't imagine the alternative.

notes: Vervaeke combines Frankfurt's notion of the unthinkable, Paul's existential ignorance, and Jung's clinical phenomenon of stuckness into one diagnostic category.

definition: The participatory way one is assuming an identity and assigning identity to the world in a co-defining process — the meta-meaning relation in which one's projects of meaning take their significance, exemplified in Fromm's having-mode versus being-mode.

sourcePassage.quote: This process by which you are co-identifying agency and arena so that they fit together and make sense of each other and you get a coherent and functioning world view... That's your existential mode.

notes: Distinct from semantic meaning; this is modal/existential meaning. Modal confusion is conflating having- and being-mode.

definition: Csikszentmihalyi's optimal experience in which skills are stretched to meet the slightly-exceeding demands of a task, producing spontaneous, effortful-yet-effortless engagement, altered sense of time and self, and an insight cascade with enhanced implicit learning.

sourcePassage.quote: the flow state is one in which the demands of the situation just slightly go beyond your skill abilities.

notes: Flow is universal across cultures. Vervaeke argues it is what shamans cultivated, and that the more often one flows, the more meaningful one rates one's life.

definition: The opposite of wisdom: not ignorance (lack of knowledge) but a lack of character such that one knows what is right yet acts otherwise — Aristotle's akrasia — a systematic failure to align salience with truth.

sourcePassage.quote: Ignorance is a lack of knowledge. Foolishness is a lack of wisdom. Foolishness is when your capacity to engage your agency, pursue your goals is undermined and threatened by the self-deception and the self-destructive behavior that is a perennial vulnerability to your cognition.

notes: For Aristotle, foolishness arises from insufficient character even when belief is correct. For Vervaeke, foolishness is endemic because the same machinery that makes us adaptive makes us self-deceptive.

definition: A set of psycho-technologies that create a ritual context — like therapy, martial arts, or Jeepform — combining enactive analogy and enactive anagoge powered by an altered (often higher) state of consciousness, by which one is liberated from existential entrapment through participatory transformation.

sourcePassage.quote: Gnosis is to have a set of psychotechnologies that create a ritual context like Jeepform, like martial arts, like therapy that allows us to overcome being existentially stuck, existentially stupefied.

notes: Vervaeke distinguishes gnosis (a salvific way of participatory knowing) from gnosticism (the historical movement and its mythology). He wants to salvage the former without the conspiracy-prone tendencies of the latter.

definition: An axial-within-axial style or movement contemporaneous with early Christianity in which the everyday world is experienced as a prison built by stupid or evil powers, from which a divine spark within us — accessed via gnosis — must be liberated by transcending the gods themselves.

sourcePassage.quote: I'm presenting the Gnostic movement as the axial revolution within the axial revolution.

notes: Gnosticism has a dark side: it can collapse into conspiracy theory, and antisemitic forms of it feed into Nazism. Vervaeke wants gnosis salvaged from gnosticism.

definition: Fromm's two existential modes: the having-mode meets having-needs by categorizing, controlling, and possessing (I-it relations); the being-mode meets being-needs by becoming, developing, and participating (I-thou relations) — confusion between them is modal confusion.

sourcePassage.quote: They're "having needs" and "being needs". So of course this mode (having needs) is called "having mode". It's an existential mode.

notes: Vervaeke argues capitalism systematically trains us into modal confusion to sell us being-mode goods (love, maturity) as having-mode commodities (cars, sex).

definition: A subset of altered states experienced as more real than ordinary reality — accompanied by clarity, oneness, ineffability, inner peace, and the demand to transform one's whole life accordingly — characterized by ontonormativity and serving as a guide for radical transformative experience.

sourcePassage.quote: reliably what is characteristic of these states is that people find them to be really real and and in both directions: Arena and Agent.

notes: 30-40 percent of the population reports having had such experiences. Vervaeke argues their rational justification lies not in propositional content but in optimizing the plausibility machinery of relevance realization.

definition: Not the fright of horror movies but the existential experience in which one's sense of grip on reality itself is undermined — when the very framing capacity by which one makes any sense is threatened — for Vervaeke a key marker of the depth of certain experiences and a function of the sacred is to home us against.

sourcePassage.quote: Most horror movies aren't horror. We'll talk about what horror really is and what people experience when they're actually experiencing horror. Their sense of grip on reality is being undermined and people find that, as you can imagine, terrifying.

notes: Vervaeke connects horror to Otto's account of the numinous as terrifying, and to Geertz's idea that sacredness homes us against horror.

definition: The cross-species adaptive mechanism that reduces the salience of distant, low-probability events — adaptive in keeping cognition tractable but a key driver of self-deceptive self-destructive behavior (smoking, procrastination) by making abstract long-term consequences invisible.

sourcePassage.quote: It's like when we talked about hyperbolic discounting. You can't do without them. They're adaptive. You need them.

notes: Vervaeke's recurring example of the 'adaptive machinery that makes us prey to self-deception' — the same cognitive structure that makes us viable also enables the cigarette-smoker's blindness.

definition: The non-conscious pickup of complex statistical patterns in the environment without explicit awareness of the rules — the cognitive mechanism behind intuition, expertise, and the participatory knowing that flow makes possible.

sourcePassage.quote: This has to do with your capacity for implicit learning. Now notice what's happening here. Notice that, although even I'm doing the history, I'm always also doing the 'cog-psy' because while I've been emphasizing the history, the historical account, I'm starting to build what I need to give you the structural functional account.

notes: Reber's 1960s work on artificial-grammar learning is the empirical foundation. Hogarth's 'educating intuition' argument extends this: intuition is the felt sense of implicit-learning output, not anything magical.

definition: The experience of having two strong motives working against each other — divided against oneself in a way that fuses with existential meaning, anxiety, and the sense of being stuck; the phenomenon Plato's tripartite psyche is designed to explain.

sourcePassage.quote: Inner conflict is when you have two strong motives that seem to be working against each other and you can see how this is immediately going to be relevant to existential meaning - to meaning in life because very often we feel most distraught, most anxious, or the most sense of being stuck when we have such inner conflict.

notes: Plato's tripartite psyche (reason / spirit / appetite) is the framework Vervaeke uses to make inner conflict cognitively tractable; akrasia and self-deception are its named failure modes.

definition: A sudden restructuring of cognition in which an inappropriate frame is broken and a better frame is constructed — disrupting an automatized pattern via scaling down and rebuilding a larger pattern via scaling up — yielding a 'flash' of consciousness and a new way of seeing.

sourcePassage.quote: I have to break up an inappropriate frame. What do I have to do? I have to break up the gestalt. And I also have to de-automatize my cognition.

notes: Vervaeke distinguishes insight in consciousness (re-framing one problem) from insight of consciousness (systematic restructuring of one's whole salience landscape — the basis of higher states of consciousness and developmental change).

definition: The crucial right-timed moment in a course of events — a turning point at which one can intervene to redirect the unfolding story — central to the Hebraic narrative ordering of time and personalized in Christianity through the figure of Jesus.

sourcePassage.quote: there's a technical term for these turning points: Kairos. It's developed by the theologian Paul Tulloch. This is this sense of the crucial turning point.

notes: Tillich's notion; Vervaeke ties Kairos to a participatory perspectival knowing of when in the unfolding course of events transformative action is possible.

definition: The Greek term for the intelligible structural-functional organization that makes a thing what it is and that makes it knowable — picked up in the Gospel of John as God's creative principle of intelligibility incarnated in Jesus.

sourcePassage.quote: The Greeks have a good word for this (S-FO): the word "logos", although a word that's being discussed a lot today. I think it needs to be discussed a little bit more carefully.

notes: Logos and mythos are not opposed in Vervaeke's account but complementary; their severance in modernity is part of the meaning crisis.

definition: A radical perspectival and participatory turning — meta- (beyond) and noia (noticing) — a fundamental transformation of one's salience landscape and modal existence, the kind of awakening Jesus and Paul speak of in the language of being born again.

sourcePassage.quote: NOIA means "noticing"; this is your perspectival awareness and "Meta" means "beyond". This means a "radical transformation in your salience landscape", a "radical transformation of what it's like to be you".

notes: Mistranslated by 'conversion' in standard Bible translations. The Christian psycho-technology for personal Kairos.

definition: Not merely paying attention to the present moment, but a set of attentional psycho-technologies — combining scaling down (meditation, Vipassana) and scaling up (contemplation, Metta) — that develop the cognitive flexibility for insight by allowing one to break inappropriate frames and construct better ones, and ultimately to recover the being-mode.

sourcePassage.quote: Mindfulness is basically teaching us how to appropriate and train a flexibility of attentional scaling so that we can intervene effectively in how we are framing our problems and increase the chances of insight when insight is needed.

notes: Vervaeke distinguishes the language of training (used pedagogically by mindfulness teachers) from the language of explaining (cognitive science). He converts mindfulness's feature list into a feature schema.

definition: The systematic conflation of being-needs with having-needs — trying to satisfy needs for love, maturity, or meaning through acquiring things rather than becoming someone — endemic in market cultures and central to existential suffering.

sourcePassage.quote: Fromm's point is we get mixed up. We try to satisfy our being needs within the having mode. We suffer from "Modal Confusion".

notes: Distinct from but related to parasitic processing. Vervaeke links modal confusion to addiction (Marc Lewis's reciprocal narrowing) and to the commercialization of identity.

definition: Not falsehood about the past but the integrated structure of symbol, story, and ritual that activates, accentuates, articulates, and celebrates religio — patterns of representation that are simultaneously patterns of interaction enabling transformation.

sourcePassage.quote: myths aren't false stories about the ancient past. They're symbolic stories about perennial patterns that are always with us.

notes: Vervaeke uses 'mythos' deliberately, distinguishing it from 'myth' in the colloquial sense. Mythos integrates symbol, story, and ritual into a single enactive system.

definition: The distinction and interdependence between mythos (symbolic, ritual, participatory patterns that articulate meaning) and logos (the rational, articulated structural-functional organization of intelligibility) — both needed; their severance is one cause of the meaning crisis.

sourcePassage.quote: The Greeks have a good word for this (S-FO): the word "logos", although a word that's being discussed a lot today. I think it needs to be discussed a little bit more carefully.

notes: John's gospel ('in the beginning was the Logos') and Hegel's translation of mythos into philosophy illustrate the convergence; the modern severance leaves logos without existential transformative relevance.

definition: The Hebraic-Christian order of meaning by which history is structured as a cosmic story with crucial turning points (Kairos), participatory faith (Da'ath), moral progress, and an open future co-created with God — what gives Western life its sense of personal journey.

sourcePassage.quote: All of this was integrated together. These three orders that articulate the space of how we're connected to ourselves, to each other and to the world. The normal logical, the normative and the narrative

notes: Marx secularizes the narrative order into political revolution; Hegel into the dialectic of Geist. The breakdown of the narrative order is also part of the meaning crisis.

definition: Plotinus's grand unification of Aristotle's worldview, Stoic therapy, and Platonic spirituality — a system in which levels of reality and levels of self ascend together, culminating in The One — and which would become the bedrock of Western spirituality.

sourcePassage.quote: Plotinus is the grand unified field theory of ancient spirituality because what does he do? Well, he takes Plato's spirituality. He takes the whole idea of anagoge from Plato. He takes Aristotle's theory, the whole Aristotle worldview, the theory of knowing, the theory of the structure of the world.

notes: Versluis and others argue Neoplatonism is the bedrock of Western spirituality. Vervaeke uses it as the paradigm of integrated science-therapy-spirituality.

definition: The collapse of meaning-making after the death of the axial-age legacy — the experience that nothing has intrinsic value, no project is genuinely meaningful, and the cosmos is indifferent — diagnosed by Schopenhauer and Nietzsche and central to the meaning crisis.

sourcePassage.quote: So here Romanticism very quickly passes into Schopenhauer (writes Schopenhauer on the board) who is the godfather of Nihilism

notes: Vervaeke describes Schopenhauer as the godfather of nihilism and Nietzsche as the godfather of postmodernism — both are responses to the loss of axial-age meaning.

definition: Vervaeke's term for a worldview in attunement that reliably affords existential modes in which agent and arena are well coupled — a cosmos in which intellectual understanding and existential dwelling converge, so individuals do not experience absurdity.

sourcePassage.quote: I'm going to call that a "Nomological Order". Nomological Order comes from no "Nomos(\*\*\*\*\*)", Law. This is what makes the universe law-like, not just in our current sense of scientific law but in the sense that it is a cosmos for us.

notes: One of three orders of meaning Vervaeke identifies — alongside the normative order (Plotinus/Platonism) and the narrative order (biblical-Hebraic). The breakdown of all three drives the meaning crisis.

definition: The order of meaning by which we can ascend in levels of being and self-knowing — the Platonic and Neoplatonic order of anagoge — that places a demand on us to grow and self-transcend.

sourcePassage.quote: From Plotinus himself, we get now what I'm going to call the Normative Order (writes Normative Order off of Plotinus and beside Aristotelian Worldview) . Plotinus gave us an account of how we can move in a coordinated fashion up the levels of reality, up the levels of consciousness, up the levels of the self, from what is less real to what is more real

notes: The normative order is what the Rhineland mystics replace with descent spirituality; what Luther rejects via sola fide; what is largely lost in the modern world.

definition: The combination of ontological force (being experienced as more real than ordinary reality) and normative demand (placing a demand on one to transform accordingly) that characterizes higher states of consciousness — making them justify rather than merely describe transformative experience.

sourcePassage.quote: So I call this... So we have a higher state of consciousness. I call this the problem of the "Ontonormativity." Remember that "ontology" has to do with the structure of reality. Normative is when things are placing a demand on you to be better, to improve.

notes: Vervaeke's neologism for the puzzle of higher states of consciousness. He resolves the puzzle by arguing they justify via optimization of plausibility machinery, not via propositional content.

definition: Merleau-Ponty's term, picked up by Dreyfus and Vervaeke, for the way an embodied agent dynamically adjusts to a situation to maximize interactional contact — neither too far (losing detail) nor too close (losing gestalt) — the basic phenomenological achievement of consciousness's relevance-realization.

sourcePassage.quote: I'm taking my salience landscape and I'm using it to get an optimal grip on things. Not maximal. And 'grip' is meant here as a metaphor. It's meant for my contact, my interactional contact.

notes: Vervaeke uses optimal grip as the phenomenological correlate of his cognitive-science account of relevance realization.

definition: Self-reinforcing dynamic patterns of cognition in which the very adaptive heuristics (representativeness, availability, confirmation bias) feed on each other and on one's affect to produce locked-in self-deceptive, self-destructive spirals — like the cognitive dynamic underlying depression or anxiety — that one cannot escape by intervening at any single point.

sourcePassage.quote: We called this in, when we published, "Parasitic Processing". It's not just about bad events. This is just one example. We get into all kinds of these spirals.

notes: Vervaeke's published account (with Ferraro). Distinct from but interpenetrating with reciprocal narrowing (Lewis). The Buddhist Eightfold Path is described as a counteractive dynamical system.

definition: The deepest kind of knowing in which you know something by identifying with it, conforming to it, and being shaped by it as you shape it — the kind of knowing involved in a relationship, a culture, or in religious or contemplative transformation.

sourcePassage.quote: There's the kind of knowing that is knowing what it's like to be in something you're participating in like a relationship.

notes: The most foundational of the four kinds of knowing. Participatory knowing is what Aristotle's conformity theory tries to capture; it is what gnosis transforms; it is what Heidegger's Dasein and 4E cognitive science articulate.

definition: The kind of knowing constituted by what it is like to occupy a particular perspective on the world — your salience landscape, your situational awareness — which cannot be transferred as propositions.

sourcePassage.quote: There's the kind of knowing of what it is like to be having this experience right now.

notes: Perspectival knowing is constituted by salience landscaping. It is the level at which transformative experiences alter what it is like to be you.

definition: The love born of cooperation and reciprocity — the love friends bear one another, in which both parties give and receive in mutual development.

sourcePassage.quote: So this is the love that is satisfied through consummation. Phylia. This is the love that seeks cooperation. This is the love in which we experienced reciprocity.

notes: Philia-sophia (friendship-love of wisdom) is the etymological root of philosophy. Distributed cognition in pursuit of wisdom is the philosophical community.

definition: The original meaning of philosophy: the friendship-love of wisdom — pursued in community via distributed cognition — contrasted with philia-nikia (love of victory), which is the disguised form of competitive argumentation that Plato repeatedly criticizes.

sourcePassage.quote: Pythagoras actually invents the word "Philosophy". It comes from two Greek words: "Philia" and "Sophia".

notes: Vervaeke recurs to philia-sophia throughout, especially in the wisdom chapters, as the cure for the modern reduction of rationality to debate.

definition: The Stoic practice of deliberately imagining loss before it occurs (kissing your child goodnight while imagining their death) — training the capacity to distinguish the physics of mortality from the meaning being made together, so love is held without dependence on permanence.

sourcePassage.quote: When you're kissing your child good night, say to yourself, "I may lose them to death tonight!". Because you have to learn to distinguish the meaning from the physics.

notes: Often misread as morbid; Vervaeke positions it as the Stoic counter to the romantic-comedy genre that re-fuses meaning and event in pernicious ways.

definition: The kind of knowing instantiated in skills — knowing how to do something, like catching a baseball or riding a bicycle — that is not reducible to propositional belief.

sourcePassage.quote: There's the kind of knowing that's involved in knowing how to catch a baseball.

notes: One of the four kinds of knowing. Procedural knowing depends on perspectival knowing (situational awareness) which depends on participatory knowing.

definition: Knowledge captured in beliefs and statements that can be true or false — the modern default of equating knowing with possessing the correct propositions.

sourcePassage.quote: We tend to have reduced all of the ways that the ancients talked about how we know to one thing: To know is to have a special kind of belief.

notes: One of the four kinds of knowing. Vervaeke argues modern culture has collapsed all knowing into the propositional, leaving us bereft of the other three kinds and contributing to the meaning crisis.

definition: The Stoic practice of paying attention to how one is paying attention — bringing the co-creation of agency and arena into awareness so as to distinguish the event from the existential meaning being assigned to it.

sourcePassage.quote: PROSOCHE is to pay attention. Now, obviously we're always paying attention, so that's useless advice! What they meant is "pay attention to how you're paying attention"

notes: Vervaeke argues this is the core of all modern cognitive psychotherapies — learning to distinguish the event from the meaning given to the event.

definition: A standardized, learnable practice — like literacy, numeracy, mindfulness, or Socratic argumentation — that fits the brain and enhances how it operates by reshaping cognition, often through internalization into metacognition.

sourcePassage.quote: A psycho-technology fits your brain and enhances how it operates.

notes: Psycho-technologies can be exapted from physical tools (literacy from oral language) and become invisible — making people forget they are culturally constructed cognitive devices.

definition: Not mere logical management of argumentation but any systematically reliable, internalized psycho-technology that affords overcoming self-deception and cultivating enhanced connectedness — culminating in the cultivation of wisdom; bound up with love and aspiration in proleptic form.

sourcePassage.quote: Rationality means any systematically reliable internalized psychotechnology that reliably and systematically affords you overcoming self-deception and affords you cultivating enhanced connectedness, enhanced meaning in life. That's why the notion of rationality I've argued for is bound up with the—it can culminate, it could point towards the cultivation of wisdom.

notes: Vervaeke critiques the modern reduction of rationality to logical inference. He follows Stanovich and develops Callard's proleptic rationality of aspiration.

definition: Marc Lewis's model of addiction: an interactive narrowing of agent and arena in which decreasing cognitive flexibility shrinks one's available options in the world, and decreasing options in the world tighten one's agency, until no alternative to the addictive pattern remains.

sourcePassage.quote: So Marc proposes a model that he calls "Reciprocal Narrowing". So here's your Agent and here's the Arena (drawn on the board). And what happens is the drug use is associated with a particular Agent:Arena relationship.

notes: The opposite of anagoge. Vervaeke and Lewis agree that the inverse direction — reciprocal opening / anagogic acceleration — is what wisdom traditions cultivate.

definition: The non-propositional, embodied, embedded process by which the mind-body dynamically selects what is relevant out of the combinatorially explosive set of available information — simultaneously a subjective coming-into-awareness and an objective making-real — the core of intelligence and the meaning-making machinery.

sourcePassage.quote: What I'm arguing is that what consciousness seems to do is the following: It seems to be a way in which you can coordinate attention and other related abilities of awareness so as to optimize how insightfully you can make sense of your world.

notes: Vervaeke's central technical construct. He proposes a naturalistic implementation in terms of self-organizing criticality and small-world network formation. RR cannot be a theory of relevance per se (lacks systematic import) but it can be a theory of the process by which relevance is realized.

definition: The deep, primordial, transjective binding of agent and arena prior to any propositional belief or institutional religion — the higher-order relevance realization by which we are connected to the world and from which all individual meaning systems derive their possibility — what cultural-religious practices have historically aimed to activate, articulate, accentuate, and celebrate.

sourcePassage.quote: We can use Mythos to activate, accelerate, articulate, and appreciate Religio. Religio is inherently valuable to us so even that alone is going to be very valuable to us.

notes: Religio is contrasted with credo (creed). The meaning crisis arises in part because we have collapsed religio into credo and lost the practices that activated religio. Religion that is not a religion would re-engage religio without metaphysical commitment.

definition: Vervaeke's distinction between religio (the lived, participatory binding to the sacred) and credo (the propositional creed) — religion proper has both, but modernity tends to reduce religion to credo, divorced from the religio that gives creeds their existential power.

sourcePassage.quote: And so I think that is something that should be acknowledged as what we would be looking for. And this brings out an important contrast (writes = beside Religio) I want to discuss (erases equal sign). Sorry, not equal. Religio versus credo

notes: Vervaeke is at pains to respect creedal religion (acknowledging the indispensability of particular mythos for particular people) while criticizing the modern collapse of religio into credo.

canonicalName: religion that is not a religion
definition: Vervaeke's name for the structural target of awakening from the meaning crisis: a community of practices, an ecology of psycho-technologies, situated within a 4E-cognitive-science worldview, that activates religio without committing to traditional metaphysical or supernatural claims.

sourcePassage.quote: we get that religion that's not a religion? And then, put that into dialogue with other people who have engaged in similar project, people like Tillich and Jung and Corbin and Barfield.

notes: Vervaeke proposes structural features: cooperative network of communities of practice in reflective equilibrium with a wisdom wiki. It is dialogically grounded in Tillich, Jung, Corbin, Barfield, and Heidegger.

definition: Vervaeke's cognitive-scientific project of analyzing what the perennial problems are and what set of practices using the machinery of relevance realization and religio can systematically address them — defining enlightenment as the developmental process that reliably ameliorates and alleviates the perennial problems.

sourcePassage.quote: I want to reverse-engineer enlightenment. I want to understand what the perennial problems are and using this machinery (indicates RR, Religio, Sacredness), what are the practices (indicates Wisdom, Enlightenment, Salvation), what are the processes we can use in order to address the perennial problems?

notes: Vervaeke explicitly stipulates this redefinition of enlightenment as the achievable cognitive-scientific target — not a superlative beyond reach but a developmental process whose mechanisms can be engineered.

definition: Not neurotic routine but enactive analogy and enactive anagoge — the structured activity by which one rehearses, in serious play, the transformation from a current self/world to a different self/world, allowing aspiration to become viable.

sourcePassage.quote: Ritual means enactive analogy, enactive Anagoge (writes ritual below mythos). It means the processes by which I try to activate the machinery of transformation.

notes: Vervaeke argues that the modern impoverishment of ritual leaves us without the structure for transformative experience, driving people toward therapy and martial arts as substitutes.

definition: The dynamically structured field of standing-out and standing-back that consciousness produces — featurization, foregrounding, configuration, and framing — by which some things become salient to me while others recede, generating the textured world in which agent and arena co-emerge.

sourcePassage.quote: Part of what's happening, part of what consciousness is doing, is it's creating a salience landscape for me.

notes: Consciousness generates a salience landscape, which sizes-up into a presence landscape (affordances), which in turn becomes a depth landscape (causal patterns). When systematically tracking truth and significance, it becomes the significance landscape.

definition: Robert Bellah's term for the metacognitive capacity that emerges when one internalizes a psycho-technology (like alphabetic literacy or numeracy) into one's reflection on one's own thinking — the cognitive substrate of the axial-age awakening to self-deception and self-correction.

sourcePassage.quote: I start to get a capacity for what [Robert Bellah](https://amzn.to/330RZAd) calls "second order thinking".

notes: Bellah's term taken up by Vervaeke as the cognitive mechanism by which alphabetic literacy and coinage made the axial revolution possible.

definition: The perennial vulnerability by which the very adaptive machinery of cognition — salience selection, heuristic shortcuts, framing — turns against the agent, producing systematic distortion that cannot be eliminated by belief alone.

sourcePassage.quote: you can't lie to yourself, but here's what I would argue: You can bullshit yourself.

notes: Vervaeke's signature thesis: the very machinery that makes you adaptive is the machinery that makes you prone to self-deceptive self-destructive behavior. This is the cognitive-scientific basis for the perennial problems.

definition: The capacity to step beyond one's current self — to overcome self-deception, expand one's frame, and grow into a more real version of oneself — that is central to the axial-age legacy and to any project of wisdom.

sourcePassage.quote: this notion of self transcendence, again which we'll have to develop and explicate, is something that's a core need because it performs core functions for human beings. It is bound up with these ideas of wisdom, and of course meaning.

notes: Westphal distinguishes epistemic and ethical self-transcendence; Vervaeke draws on this distinction to criticize Hegel for offering the first while leaving out the second.

definition: John Wright's term (drawing on Iris Murdoch) for the bi-directional transformation in which one both perceives the world differently and becomes a different perceiver — the cognitive achievement underlying viable entry into a new worldview.

sourcePassage.quote: Wright calls this Sensibility Transcendence. Because what's happening, simultaneously, is the daughter-in-law can be something that she couldn't be before and the mother-in-law is becoming somebody she couldn't become before; she couldn't be before.

notes: Wright's term, drawn from Murdoch's The Sovereignty of Good. Vervaeke treats sensibility transcendence as virtually synonymous with anagoge.

definition: The mode of engagement in which one is between the world one is in and the world one aspires to — neither fully one nor the other — playing in earnest with possibilities of transformation, exemplified in role-play, Tai Chi, therapy with empty-chair technique, and ritual.

sourcePassage.quote: are people doing in all these instances there? They're confronting the possibility of a transformative experience. And so what they do is they have an enactive analogy.

notes: Vervaeke argues recovering serious play is essential to addressing the meaning crisis — one of the core capacities lost in the collapse of traditional religion.

definition: The set of psycho-technologies — disruptive strategies (sleep deprivation, chanting, dance, isolation, psychedelics) combined with metaphorical enactment — by which Upper-Paleolithic practitioners exapted the cognitive enhancements of trade and initiation rituals to alter consciousness, generate insight cascades, and access powerful implicit learning.

sourcePassage.quote: Shamanism is a set of psycho-technologies for altering your state of consciousness and enhancing your cognition.

notes: Vervaeke argues shamanism is plausibly what drove the Upper Paleolithic transition — a software change in cognition rather than a biological one.

definition: The ancient Greek excellence of soundness of mind and character — the well-balanced person whose salience naturally tracks the good — for Vervaeke a key trait dimension in his theory of wisdom, where the salience landscape is so well cultivated that one is reliably oriented toward truth.

sourcePassage.quote: we talked about Sophrosyne. When you become an adult, your world becomes radically reoriented. What is salient and what is central to you is radically changed.

notes: Vervaeke uses sophrosyne in his wisdom theory as the trait outcome of well-trained salience landscaping.

definition: Aristotle's idos translated into modern terms: not the matter of a thing but the pattern by which its parts are integrated to function as a whole — what makes a bird a bird beyond beak, wings, and feathers — and the pattern by which the mind comes to know it.

sourcePassage.quote: What's missing from this is the "Structural-Functional Organization" that makes the whole greater than the sum of its parts.

notes: Vervaeke uses SFO as the modern translation of Plato's idos and Aristotle's form — central to his theory of how mindfulness converts a feature list into a feature schema.

definition: Plato's metaphor of the sun outside the cave — that which is the source both of the realness of things and of the mind's capacity to know them — Plotinus calls this The One, the principle of intelligibility and being beyond all particular things.

sourcePassage.quote: And what are they looking for? They want to see the source of the real light. The light that's allowing them to pick up on the real patterns.

notes: Iris Murdoch's Sovereignty of Good is invoked as the modern philosophical recovery of this Platonic notion; Vervaeke ties it to transframing and aspirational rationality.

definition: Plato's integration of Socratic self-knowledge and Pythagorean self-transcendence into a developmental program: by ordering the psyche so the man teaches the lion which tames the monster, we simultaneously reduce inner conflict and come into deeper contact with reality, anagogically ascending from the cave through the forms to the Good.

sourcePassage.quote: Plato describes wisdom as an internal Justice within the psyche in which the man has been taught, the line has been trained and the monster has been tamed so they can get along together as much as is possible.

notes: Vervaeke treats Plato (together with the Bible) as one of the two foundational feet of Western civilization. The Platonic project keeps reverberating through Plotinus, Augustine, Neoplatonism, and into modern psychology.

canonicalName: the True, the Good, and the Beautiful
definition: The Platonic and medieval triad of normative-aspirational orientations — toward truth, goodness, and beauty — that for Vervaeke jointly anchor the inherently transjective valuations of the sacred and the cultivation of wisdom.

sourcePassage.quote: I'm losing truth. I'm losing goodness. I'm losing beauty. I'm losing what makes things to Be and what makes them to be sensible and intelligible.

notes: Augustine puts the love of the True, Good, and Beautiful at the center of his integration of Neoplatonism with Christianity.

definition: The radical change in human cognition around 40,000 BCE — manifest in art, music, projectile weapons, abstract symbols, calendars — driven not by hardware change in the brain but by a software change in how humans were using their brains, plausibly powered by shamanism as an ecology of psycho-technologies.

sourcePassage.quote: So this period is known as the Upper Paleolithic transition. It occurs around 40000 BCE. Now what's interesting about this is that biologically as a species we've existed much longer than this.

notes: The first of the great cognitive transitions Vervaeke uses to frame his history of meaning-making.

definition: The pivotal cultural transformation from around 800 BCE to 300 BCE in Greece, Israel, India, and China when, following the Bronze Age collapse, new psycho-technologies — alphabetic literacy, coinage, second-order thinking — generated an awareness of self-transcendence and self-deception, replacing the continuous cosmos with a two-worlds mythology in which wisdom became the means of moving from the illusory everyday world to the more real world.

sourcePassage.quote: around 800 BCE to around 300 B.C. there's this great change such that you will read, connect to, find relevant authors, systems of thought, ways of being from that time period.

notes: Vervaeke treats the Axial Revolution as the formative event for Western/world civilization, comparable in significance to the Upper Paleolithic transition for our humanity itself.

definition: Charles Taylor's term for the pre-axial Bronze Age worldview in which there is deep continuity between the natural, cultural, and divine worlds — differences are differences of power, not kind — and time moves in great repeating cycles rather than as a story.

sourcePassage.quote: So in the Bronze Age world, before the axial revolution, people experienced the world in what's been called by Charles Taylor "the continuous cosmos"

notes: The shift from the continuous cosmos to the two-worlds mythology is the axial revolution's central restructuring of meaning. The scientific revolution then ironically returns us to a 'continuous cosmos' in which nothing is qualitatively distinct.

definition: Tillich's existential virtue of facing the threats of meaninglessness, non-being, and despair — not by retreating into false certainty but by living the perspectival, participatory courage to act in the face of doubt — for Vervaeke a model of the existential response to the meaning crisis.

sourcePassage.quote: The brave person faces danger, but that's all we can say about them. The person with fortitude endures difficulty, but that's all we can say of them. The courageous person sees through the illusion and the distortion of fear or distress to what is truly good and acts accordingly.

notes: Tillich's title book is repeatedly invoked. Vervaeke treats it as the modern recovery of the axial virtue of facing dukkha without religious certainty.

definition: Plato's model of the psyche as three centers — reason (the man, oriented to truth and long-term abstract goals), spirit/thymos (the lion, oriented to honor and shared cultural meaning), and appetite (the monster, oriented to short-term pleasure and pain) — whose mutual conflict produces self-deception and whose proper ordering is wisdom.

sourcePassage.quote: What you want to do is get it properly ordered. When you don't order it, think about what this means... Salience and understanding and participation get out of sync with each other.

notes: Vervaeke argues this division keeps being rediscovered — Freud's id/ego/superego; the triune-brain model — because Plato got something deeply right about hyperbolic discounting and motivational hierarchy.

definition: Corbin's symbol from Persian Sufism for the better self that shines into one's current frame from a more encompassing frame — internalizing the better self enables one to transframe into indwelling that larger world, integrating abstract aspiration with concrete present action.

sourcePassage.quote: The divine double allows you to internalize from this more encompassing frame into your current frame (draws an arrow from the large square to the smaller square and writes DD) but that is simultaneously—and here's the shining in (indicates the arrow). Here's the shining in through the divine double.

notes: Drawn from Persian Sufism via Corbin. Vervaeke connects the divine double to Callard's account of aspiration.

definition: The Buddha's counteractive dynamical system — right understanding, right thinking, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right concentration — operating simultaneously at the level of cognition, character, and consciousness to counter parasitic processing and afford reciprocal opening.

sourcePassage.quote: The Eightfold Path is a counteractive dynamical system that counteracts parasitic processing and does reciprocal opening, beyond the ego self and beyond the everyday world where it's represented by an eight spoked wheel.

notes: Vervaeke uses the Eightfold Path as the paradigm of a coordinated ecology of psycho-technologies functioning as a counteractive dynamical system.

definition: Vervaeke's taxonomy of four irreducible kinds of knowing — propositional, procedural, perspectival, and participatory — where the propositional depends on the procedural, the procedural on the perspectival, and the perspectival on the participatory.

sourcePassage.quote: we are getting a lot about how we should think about Relevance Realisation where we should look for it and notice it’s starting to give us a way of connecting and thinking about the four kinds of knowing.

notes: Asymmetric dependence is essential: you cannot have higher kinds without the lower kinds, but the lower kinds are non-reducible to propositional knowing. This grounds the rejection of belief-centric models of meaning and religion.

definition: The foundational problem in AI and cognitive science of how an agent zeroes in on relevant information given the combinatorial explosion of possible considerations — solved in human cognition by relevance realization, not by exhaustive search.

sourcePassage.quote: If this is the whole problem space (draws one big circle) — this is what you do somehow — you can't search the whole space

notes: Vervaeke uses the frame problem as the motivating problem for relevance realization. He argues no propositional inference can solve it because the space is combinatorially explosive.

aliases: God beyond the God of theism
definition: Tillich's name for the inexhaustible source of intelligibility and being that is presupposed by any particular theism and that survives the collapse of theistic mythology — Vervaeke's connection to The One, to the inexhaustible sacred, and to religio.

sourcePassage.quote: talks about that the response to that is a way of discovering, and it's clear because of how he talks about symbols and he talks about transformation, although he does not use the term, but he's talking about gnosis. He's talking about that the response to the meaning crisis is the gnosis of the God beyond the God of theism.

notes: Vervaeke treats Tillich's ground of being as a viable secular reformulation of what theistic religions traditionally pointed at.

definition: Corbin's term for the transjective use of image that mediates between the abstract intelligible world and the concrete sensible world — distinct from the imaginary (subjective mental pictures) — in an ongoing transformative transframing that is the cognitive heart of the symbol.

sourcePassage.quote: imaginal which mediates between the abstract intelligible world and the concrete, sensible world and transjectively mediates between the subjective and the objective.

notes: Corbin's coinage from his work on Sufism and Persian philosophy. Vervaeke uses the imaginal to argue for a non-supernatural retrieval of religious imagination.

definition: A pervasive cultural-historical phenomenon in which modern people experience a profound sense of disconnection — from themselves, each other, the world, and a viable future — and a loss of the cognitive and existential resources by which earlier cultures generated meaning, manifesting in rising suicide rates, mental-health collapse, nihilism, and the sense of drowning in bullshit.

sourcePassage.quote: This is gonna be an idea that our culture is experiencing a profound "meaning crisis". A "crisis in meaning".

notes: Vervaeke distinguishes two interlocked drivers: (1) historical factors that have eroded the Western worldview and its psycho-technologies, and (2) perennial problems endemic to the very machinery that makes us adaptive. Awakening from the meaning crisis therefore requires both worldview rearticulation and an ecology of practices.

definition: Plato's parable in which prisoners chained to face shadows on a cave wall mistake them for reality until one breaks free, sees the fire, ascends to the surface, and glimpses the sun (the Good) — the paradigmatic image of anagoge, of moving from illusion to reality through self-transformation.

sourcePassage.quote: This (Myth of the Cave) is a myth of enlightenment: of coming into the light. It's a myth of self-transcendence and self-transformation.

notes: Recurs throughout the series as the paradigm myth — explicitly resurfaced in The Matrix, in Gnostic mythology, and in any narrative of awakening.

definition: Otto's name for the experience of the sacred as simultaneously fascinating (super-salient, drawing one in) and terrifying (transgressing one's frame, humbling one), with mystery as its core — bordering on horror and yet integral to sacredness.

sourcePassage.quote: Otto describes this experience of the numinous as having three central aspects to it. It is a mystery (writes mystery off of the numinous) very much in the way I argued last time

notes: Otto's term used by Vervaeke to extend Geertz's notion of the sacred as homing against horror — the sacred not only protects from horror but transgresses through it.

definition: The cross-cultural, transhistorical vulnerabilities — modal confusion, reciprocal narrowing, parasitic processing, existential entrapment, absurdity, alienation, anxiety — endemic to the very relevance-realization machinery that makes us adaptively intelligent; the second leg of the meaning crisis alongside historical factors.

sourcePassage.quote: I want to argue that all cultures, all people – all people in the sense of being participants in their culture, not necessarily every single individual – but all cultures, across time, place, history, right? People are prone to perennial problems. These are ways in which the machinery of Relevance Realization can drive them into meaninglessness, despair.

notes: Cultures cultivate ecologies of psycho-technologies (typically religions) to address the perennial problems. The meaning crisis is what happens when historical factors have invalidated those traditions, leaving the perennial problems uncountered.

canonicalName: the prophets of the meaning crisis
definition: Vervaeke's term for the modern thinkers — Nietzsche, Heidegger, Tillich, Jung, Corbin, Barfield — who diagnosed the meaning crisis and pointed toward responses, used in the Old Testament sense of prophet (one who tells forth, who wakes us up to how we are off course).

sourcePassage.quote: This is ultimately going to come back to the dialogue that I'm going to set up between what I've argued for here and some of the other profits of this religion beyond a religion, that's not a religion like, Tillich, Jung —I would argue, also Corbin and perhaps aspects of Barfield, and the godfather of all of that, of course, are people like, Heidegger.

notes: Episode 46 frames the final stretch of the series as a sustained dialogue with these prophets.

definition: The transjective relationship between the inexhaustible no-thing-ness of reality (combinatorially explosive, never finally framed) and the inexhaustible no-thing-ness of relevance realization in oneself — what mythos activates and serious play celebrates — not the supernatural but the deep ground of meaning making.

sourcePassage.quote: The sacred is the Transjective relationship between the combinatorially explosive nature of Reality - that reality is ultimately a no-thing-ness, that ultimately it is not a thing that you can frame. Reality will always transcend your framing.

notes: Vervaeke distinguishes the sacred from the supernatural and proposes a naturalistic account that nonetheless does justice to the function and phenomenology of sacredness. Plato as inexhaustible font is his exemplar.

definition: Pascal's name for the perspectival, participatory mode of knowing that grasps a situation as a whole through caring engagement — contrasted with the spirit of geometry (esprit de géométrie), the propositional inferential mode — the loss of which leaves us with science but without transformation.

sourcePassage.quote: And so Pascal is pointing out that what has been lost in the scientific revolution is all these other kinds of knowing and being, and these are the kinds of knowing and being that he found present in the transformative experience that he had.

notes: Pascal's diagnosis of the modern problem: Descartes's method excludes the spirit of finesse precisely the kind of knowing needed for existential transformation.

definition: Not a sign that merely points to a referent but a transjective participatory structure that bridges the world I am in and the world I aspire to — triggering transframing by drawing me into a non-logical identity between my current frame and a more encompassing one.

sourcePassage.quote: symbols are inherently transjective (indicates the dynamic in Fig.6) because they are between two worlds: the world you’re in/the framing you're in and the world you're trying to move to/the world that you aspire to. They are transgressive in all the ways I've tried to suggest to you. They try to trigger transframing. They're transformative.

notes: Vervaeke develops the symbol via Tillich and Corbin, then ties it to the imaginal (Corbin's coinage) — the ongoing transformative transframing mediated by image.

definition: L.A. Paul's category of experiences (e.g., becoming a vampire, having a child) that change both who you are (personal transformation) and what you know about being that way (epistemic transformation), so that you cannot reason your way through whether to undergo them — you must aspire or refuse from the position of radical existential ignorance.

sourcePassage.quote: you don't know what it is like cause that's the core of you don't know what your salience landscape will be like when you eat this fruit until you have eaten the fruit.

notes: Vervaeke uses Paul's framework to explain why higher states of consciousness justify rather than describe — they reshape one's perspectival and participatory knowing.

definition: A bi-directional, participatory transformation that simultaneously reframes how one sees the world and reframes how one sees oneself — a systematic restructuring of the whole framing process rather than the reframing of any particular problem.

sourcePassage.quote: It's not a reframing of a particular problem. It's a transformation of the whole framing process: Both ends.

notes: Vervaeke's neologism. The Murdoch mother-in-law example is canonical: she does not merely reframe her daughter-in-law but transframes herself in the process. Transframing is the cognitive signature of wisdom and of sacred symbolic engagement.

definition: The mode of relation that is neither merely subjective (projected by the mind) nor merely objective (a property of the thing), but a real co-constituting relation between organism and environment — agent and arena — in which both poles are mutually realized.

sourcePassage.quote: I want to propose a term to you. I want to argue that relevance is in this sense, transjective (writes this on the board). It is a real relationship between the organism and its environment. We should not think of it as being projected. We should not think of it as being detected.

notes: The transjective is Vervaeke's primary metaphysical category for moving beyond the subjective/objective dichotomy. Symbols, religio, and the sacred are all transjective phenomena.

definition: The axial-age mythological structure in which an illusory everyday world (the world of self-deception and suffering) is contrasted with a more real world (the world disclosed to the trained, wise mind), with wisdom understood as the process of moving from one to the other.

sourcePassage.quote: Now what's this new world? Well, this new mythological world view uses a mythology of two worlds. The idea is "this" is the "Everyday World". This is the world of the untrained mind.

notes: Vervaeke insists this is mythological, not metaphysical: most axial-age thinkers used it as a model for self-transformation, not a literal claim about two realities.

definition: The Stoic practice of altering the level of construal — situating an event within ever-wider frames of space and time (room → city → world → cosmos through all time) to shift how one relates to it, altering the sense of self by changing perspective.

sourcePassage.quote: What's another practice that the Stoics engage in? A practice they engaged in is called "the view from above".

notes: Closely tied to what later literature calls the Solomon Effect — moving from first- to third-person perspective on one's own situation, with measurable cognitive consequences.

definition: Not a single behavior but a cultivated set of conditions — Aristotle's golden mean between deficiency and excess — that systematically shapes possibility in a person, forming the virtual engine that regulates their development toward actualizing their potential.

sourcePassage.quote: When you're talking about a virtue you're not talking about an event. You're talking about, again, a set of conditions that have been cultivated systematically in somebody.

notes: Vervaeke connects virtue (Latin virtus) to virtual engine — the regulating system of constraints on one's self-development.

definition: The dynamical ecology of psycho-technologies and cognitive styles that reliably counteracts self-deceptive self-destructive processing and affords the systematic seeing through illusion and into reality — a transframing competence that enhances religio, meaning in life, and the cultivation of virtue.

sourcePassage.quote: We're seeing that wisdom is a dynamical system. A dynamical system that is counteractive to the machinery of self-deception. And that helps to afford the self-organized transformation into the life of flourishing.

notes: Vervaeke's mature account integrates Ardelt's three-factor model, Baltes & Staudinger's Berlin paradigm, Sternberg's balance theory, and Schwartz & Sharpe's practical wisdom — with relevance realization as the connective tissue across them.

definition: The ongoing process by which one's account of the world and one's account of knowing the world mutually support each other in a coherent dynamic — generating the existential modes in which agent and arena co-identify successfully.

sourcePassage.quote: the way you get this mutual support, mutual intelligibility - not as a static relation but as an unfolding process. He calls this "Worldview Attunement".

notes: Geertz's term, picked up by Vervaeke for what is at stake when a worldview collapses — the scientific revolution undid Aristotelian worldview attunement and we have not built a successor.
